directions.name: Pairwise unroll the peeled 64x384 hot loop without changing the handoff model
directions.hypothesis: The restored 2872f92 winner already proved that deeper single-skew overlap inside the peeled 64x384 hot kernel is useful, but it still only reaches 33.64% tensor active with 15.46% barrier stall and 29.92% mio_throttle. The 3eeb098 named-barrier/subgroup stage-handoff retune is strong negative evidence for adding more handoff state because it dropped the hot kernel to one register-limited block per SM at 167 registers/thread. The best bounded follow-up is to keep the accepted cp.async plus CTA barrier recycle model and process two fixed K tiles per steady-state iteration so the hot loop pays less loop/index overhead per MMA issue.
directions.expected_bottleneck: Tensor issue underfill and loop/control overhead inside the fixed 64x384 hot kernel, with register growth as the main guardrail.
directions.risk: A 2x steady-state unroll can recreate the recent register blow-up; if the hot kernel climbs well above the accepted 128 registers/thread, occupancy will collapse back to the failure mode seen at 3eeb098.
directions.stop_condition: Stop if the hot kernel falls back to one register-limited block per SM, or if tensor active fails to improve while end-to-end runtime stays flat or regresses.

directions.name: Add a fixed-K peeled 64x96 tail kernel
directions.hypothesis: The accepted 64x384 hot band now dominates total time, but the remaining 64x96 tail still runs through the generic tensor-core loop and shows about 19.9% barrier stall plus 15.3% short scoreboard in both the winner and the bad follow-up. Reusing the peeled fixed-K schedule and the trimmed quad export path on the final 96 columns is a bounded way to harvest the same fixed-shape win on the only remaining generic slice, without reopening the failed named-barrier/subgroup handoff path or disturbing the restored 2872f92 hot kernel.
directions.expected_bottleneck: Residual barrier and scoreboard overhead in the generic 64x96 tail kernel; total upside is capped because the tail is only about 0.9 ms of GPU time today.
directions.risk: The tail is a small slice of total runtime, so even a clear tail-kernel win may only move the end-to-end median by a few tenths of a millisecond.
directions.stop_condition: Stop if the specialized tail does not materially reduce tail-kernel duration or if the total runtime movement is within measurement noise.

directions.name: Micro-retune the single-level B skew inside the existing 64x384 pipeline
directions.hypothesis: The restored 2872f92 base still carries 29.92% mio_throttle in the hot kernel, so there may be a little headroom left in the existing B shared layout. But round-1 two-level B repacking and the latest named-barrier/subgroup handoff are both negative evidence against broader feed-path rewrites. The only coherent follow-up here is a tiny change to the current single-level skew and logical-to-shared column mapping, keeping the same cp.async producer path and the same consumer fragment schedule.
directions.expected_bottleneck: Shared-memory bank behavior and LSU pressure on B-fragment loads, with limited headroom before a mapping tweak starts hurting cp.async coalescing or reopens earlier feed-path regressions.
directions.risk: Feed-path experiments have mostly regressed, so this must stay a one-constant or one-mapping tweak only. If it starts turning into another repacking rewrite, it is repeating disproven ground.
directions.stop_condition: Stop if bank or mio metrics move without a runtime win, or if the change requires a second staging level or any new synchronization state.

notes: Diagnosed run 20260419_103438_bf16_gemm_v1_3eeb098 against the restored accepted base 2872f92. The named-barrier/subgroup stage-handoff retune is strong negative evidence: it cut barrier and mio stalls but ballooned the hot kernel to 167 registers/thread, dropped register-limited occupancy to one block per SM, and regressed to 41.745407 ms. The ranking therefore stays on bounded changes around the winning peeled 64x384 plus 64x96 split and does not continue the named-barrier path.
